seoTitle: How to Add a Secondary Axis to a Power BI Chart
metaDescription: Add a secondary axis to a clustered column chart in Power BI so you can compare metrics with very different scales on one visual.
intro: Comparing two metrics with very different scales, like revenue and growth percentage, is hard on a single-axis chart. This guide shows how to add a secondary axis to a clustered column chart in Power BI so both metrics stay readable side by side. It's a practical technique for anyone building comparison-heavy business reports.

steps.heading: Build the base clustered column chart
steps.body: Start by creating a standard clustered column chart with the fields you want to compare before adjusting the axis settings.

steps.heading: Add the secondary axis
steps.body: In the chart's formatting options, assign one of your measures to a secondary axis so it's plotted on its own scale.

steps.heading: Customize the chart for clarity
steps.body: Adjust colors, labels, and axis titles so it's clear which series belongs to the primary axis and which belongs to the secondary one.

steps.heading: Apply best practices for dual-axis reading
steps.body: Keep the number of series limited and label both axes clearly so viewers can interpret the comparison without confusion.

steps.heading: Use it in a real business comparison
steps.body: Apply the technique to a scenario like revenue versus expenses or sales versus growth metrics to see the practical benefit.

faq.question: Why would I use a secondary axis instead of two separate charts?
faq.answer: A secondary axis lets viewers compare two related metrics on the same visual, which can reveal relationships that separate charts might obscure.

faq.question: Can I use a secondary axis on any chart type in Power BI?
faq.answer: Secondary axes are commonly used with clustered column and line charts, though not every visual type supports this option.

faq.question: Will a secondary axis make my chart harder to read?
faq.answer: It can if overused, so it's best applied when comparing exactly two metrics with clearly different scales, with clear axis labeling.

faq.question: Can I combine a column chart and a line chart using a secondary axis?
faq.answer: Yes, a common approach is a line-and-clustered-column chart where the line series uses the secondary axis.

faq.question: How many measures can I put on a secondary axis?
faq.answer: You can technically add more than one, but keeping it to a single measure per axis usually keeps the chart easiest to interpret.
